era_context: The early 18th century was defined by the War of the Spanish Succession (1701–1714), which reshaped the European balance of power as the Bourbon and Habsburg dynasties contested the Spanish throne. Russia under Peter the Great was asserting itself as a major European power, clashing with both Sweden in the Great Northern War and the Ottoman Empire. The Qing Dynasty under the Kangxi Emperor was at its zenith in China, while the Mughal Empire under Bahadur Shah I was beginning its long decline. In the Americas, European colonial powers were expanding and increasingly coming into conflict with Indigenous nations.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa beyond coastal trading posts
Indigenous South American societies beyond Iberian colonial records

events.title: Pruth River Campaign and Treaty
events.description: Peter the Great led a Russian army southward against the Ottoman Empire, expecting support from Moldavian and Wallachian princes. The campaign ended in disaster when a far larger Ottoman force under Grand Vizier Baltacı Mehmet Pasha surrounded the Russian army at the Pruth River in July 1711. Facing annihilation, Peter negotiated the Treaty of the Pruth, surrendering the fortress of Azov and its access to the Sea of Azov, and agreeing to demolish fortifications along the southern frontier. The defeat reversed a decade of Russian gains against the Ottomans and temporarily redirected Peter's expansionist ambitions back toward the Baltic.
key_figures: Catherine I (Peter's consort, present at the campaign)

sources.name: Treaty of the Pruth, diplomatic records preserved in Ottoman and Russian archives
Peter the Great's campaign correspondence and military records
Brian Davies, The Russo-Turkish War, 1768–1774 (contextual background on Pruth legacy)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in both Russian and Ottoman contemporary sources, with the treaty text itself surviving.

events.title: Battle of Bouchain — Marlborough's final major victory
events.description: The Duke of Marlborough captured the heavily fortified town of Bouchain in September 1711 through a daring siege operation, outmaneuvering the French Marshal Villars's defensive lines (the Ne Plus Ultra lines). It was considered one of Marlborough's most skilful operations, achieved without a pitched battle through superior logistics and engineering. This was Marlborough's last significant military action before his political dismissal by Queen Anne's government at the end of 1711, marking the effective end of Britain's aggressive prosecution of the War of the Spanish Succession.
key_figures: John Churchill, 1st Duke of Marlborough (Allied commander)
Claude Louis Hector de Villars (French Marshal)

sources.name: David Chandler, Marlborough as Military Commander
Contemporary dispatches and military correspondence from the campaign

events.certainty_note: Well-documented military operation with contemporary dispatches, maps, and accounts from both sides.

events.title: Dismissal of Marlborough and British peace pivot
events.description: Queen Anne, influenced by the Tory ministry led by Robert Harley (Earl of Oxford) and Henry St John (Viscount Bolingbroke), dismissed the Duke of Marlborough from all his offices on 31 December 1711. The Tory government had been conducting secret peace negotiations with France, and Marlborough's removal signaled Britain's definitive turn toward ending the War of the Spanish Succession. This political shift had profound consequences for European diplomacy, as it meant the Grand Alliance against France would lose its most effective military commander and most committed participant.
key_figures: Robert Harley, Earl of Oxford (Lord Treasurer)
Henry St John, Viscount Bolingbroke (Secretary of State)

sources.name: London Gazette and parliamentary records, 1711
Edward Gregg, Queen Anne (biography)

events.certainty_note: Precisely dated political event documented in official records and extensive contemporary correspondence.

events.title: South Sea Company founded
events.description: The South Sea Company was established by an Act of Parliament in 1711, promoted by Robert Harley as a financial scheme to consolidate and manage approximately £9 million of government debt. The company was granted a monopoly on British trade with Spanish South America and the Pacific, though this trade never materialised in significant volume. The company's formation was an important innovation in public finance and government debt management, but it would become infamous for the catastrophic speculative bubble of 1720 (the South Sea Bubble), one of the first major financial crises of the modern era.
key_figures: John Blunt (company director and chief architect of the scheme)

sources.name: Act of Parliament establishing the South Sea Company, 1711 (9 Anne c. 21)
John Carswell, The South Sea Bubble

events.certainty_note: Founding documented in parliamentary legislation and extensive contemporary records.

events.title: Tuscarora War begins in North Carolina
events.region: British colonial North Carolina (modern eastern North Carolina, USA)
events.description: In September 1711, Tuscarora warriors and allied groups launched coordinated attacks on colonial settlements along the Neuse and Pamlico Rivers in retaliation for years of encroachment, enslavement of Indigenous people, and abuses by colonial traders. The initial attacks killed approximately 130 settlers in what colonists called the 'Bath County Massacre.' The war continued until 1713, ending with the defeat of the Tuscarora, many of whom were killed or enslaved, with survivors eventually migrating north to join the Iroquois Confederacy as the Sixth Nation. The conflict reshaped Indigenous-colonial relations in the southeastern colonies.
key_figures: Christopher von Graffenried (Swiss colonist captured during attacks)

sources.name: Christopher von Graffenried's account of his captivity (published later but based on 1711 experience)
Colonial records of North Carolina
David La Vere, The Tuscarora War: Indians, Settlers, and the Fight for the Carolina Colonies

events.certainty_note: Well-documented in colonial records, though primarily from the settler perspective. Tuscarora perspectives are reconstructed from indirect evidence.

events.title: French raid on Rio de Janeiro
events.description: French privateer and naval commander René Duguay-Trouin led a large expedition of 17 ships and approximately 5,700 men that captured and sacked Rio de Janeiro in September 1711, exploiting Portuguese weakness during the War of the Spanish Succession. The French held the city for ransom, extracting a massive payment in gold, sugar, and cattle before withdrawing. The raid exposed the vulnerability of Portugal's most important colonial port and prompted significant Portuguese investment in Rio de Janeiro's fortifications. This was the last successful large-scale foreign attack on a major Brazilian coastal city.
key_figures: Francisco de Castro Morais (Portuguese colonial governor)

sources.name: René Duguay-Trouin, Mémoires (published 1740, based on his journals)
Portuguese colonial administrative correspondence, Arquivo Histórico Ultramarino

events.certainty_note: Documented extensively by both French and Portuguese contemporary sources.

events.title: The Spectator begins publication
events.description: Joseph Addison and Richard Steele launched The Spectator on 1 March 1711, a daily periodical essay that became one of the most influential publications in English literary history. Published daily until December 1712 (with a brief revival in 1714), it reached an estimated readership of 60,000 per issue through coffeehouses and shared copies. The Spectator helped shape English prose style, promoted Enlightenment values of politeness, reason, and moral reflection, and established the periodical essay as a major literary form. Its influence extended throughout Europe and to the American colonies.

sources.name: The Spectator, original issues 1711–1712
Donald F. Bond (ed.), The Spectator (critical edition)

events.certainty_note: First issue dated 1 March 1711; complete run survives.

events.title: Occasional Conformity Act passed
events.description: Parliament passed the Occasional Conformity Act in December 1711, prohibiting the practice by which Protestant Dissenters (Presbyterians, Baptists, Quakers, etc.) occasionally took Anglican communion to qualify for public office under the Test Acts. The act imposed fines and disqualification on officeholders who attended Dissenting worship services. It represented a Tory-High Church political victory and narrowed religious toleration in Britain, though enforcement was inconsistent. The act was repealed in 1719 under the Whig ascendancy.
key_figures: Robert Harley, Earl of Oxford (political sponsor)
Henry Sacheverell (High Church advocate whose 1709 trial catalysed Tory revival)

sources.name: Parliamentary records and statutes, 10 Anne c. 6
Geoffrey Holmes, The Trial of Doctor Sacheverell (contextual background)

events.certainty_note: Statutory record with precise date and parliamentary debate documentation.

events.title: Failed British expedition against Quebec
events.region: British North America / New France (St. Lawrence River)
events.description: An ambitious British naval expedition under Admiral Sir Hovenden Walker, carrying approximately 5,000 troops and 6,000 sailors, sailed up the St. Lawrence River to capture Quebec from the French. The expedition ended in disaster when eight transport ships were wrecked on rocks near Île-aux-Oeufs in fog on the night of 22–23 August, drowning approximately 900 soldiers and sailors. Walker abandoned the campaign and returned to England, where the debacle was received with disgust. The failure preserved New France for another half-century and demonstrated the extreme navigational hazards of the St. Lawrence approach.
key_figures: Philippe de Rigaud, Marquis de Vaudreuil (Governor of New France)

sources.name: Hovenden Walker, A Journal, or Full Account of the Late Expedition to Canada (1720)
Gerald Graham, The Walker Expedition to Quebec, 1711 (Champlain Society)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented through Walker's own account, Admiralty records, and French reports.

events.title: Death of Emperor Joseph I and Habsburg succession crisis
events.description: Holy Roman Emperor Joseph I died of smallpox on 17 April 1711 at age 32, creating a dynastic crisis that fundamentally altered the War of the Spanish Succession. His brother Charles, who was the Allied candidate for the Spanish throne, now inherited the Austrian Habsburg lands and was elected Holy Roman Emperor as Charles VI in October 1711. This made the Allied war aim of placing Charles on the Spanish throne far less attractive to Britain and the Dutch Republic, as it would recreate the feared union of Spain and the Empire under one ruler — the very concentration of power the war was meant to prevent. Joseph's death was thus a pivotal factor in the eventual Peace of Utrecht.
key_figures: Charles VI (brother and successor as Emperor)

sources.name: Court records and diplomatic correspondence from Vienna, 1711
Charles Ingrao, In Quest and Crisis: Emperor Joseph I and the Habsburg Monarchy

events.certainty_note: Precisely dated death with extensive contemporary documentation across European courts.

events.title: Arai Hakuseki's Shōtoku reforms in Tokugawa Japan
events.description: Following the accession of Shogun Tokugawa Ienobu in 1709, his Confucian advisor Arai Hakuseki continued implementing the Shōtoku reforms in 1711, which aimed to restore fiscal discipline and governmental prestige. Key measures included restricting the debased currency that had been introduced under the previous shogunate, reforming diplomatic protocols (notably restructuring relations with Korea and the Ryukyu Kingdom), and tightening trade restrictions at Nagasaki to reduce gold and silver outflows. Hakuseki's intellectual approach to governance, emphasising rationalism and documentary evidence, represented a significant moment in Japanese political thought.

sources.name: Arai Hakuseki, Oritaku Shiba no Ki (Told Round a Brushwood Fire, autobiography)
Kate Wildman Nakai, Shogunal Politics: Arai Hakuseki and the Premises of Tokugawa Rule

events.certainty_note: Well-documented through Hakuseki's own extensive writings and bakufu records.

events.title: Death of Mughal Emperor Bahadur Shah I
events.description: Mughal Emperor Bahadur Shah I (also known as Shah Alam I) died on 27 February 1712, though his health had been declining severely through late 1711, making much of that year a period of political uncertainty and factional maneuvering. His death triggered a violent war of succession among his four sons, which was won by Jahandar Shah with the support of the kingmaker Zulfiqar Khan. Bahadur Shah's reign (1707–1712) had been the last period of relative stability for the Mughal Empire; the succession wars after his death accelerated the empire's fragmentation.

sources.name: Khafi Khan, Muntakhab al-Lubab (contemporary chronicle)
William Irvine, Later Mughals (edited by Jadunath Sarkar)

events.certainty_note: Date of death is in early 1712 but the political crisis began in 1711. Contemporary Mughal chronicles document the period extensively.

events.title: Kangxi Emperor's southern inspection tour
events.description: The Kangxi Emperor undertook his sixth and final southern inspection tour (nanxun) in 1711, traveling through the Jiangnan region, the economic heartland of the Qing Empire. These tours served multiple purposes: demonstrating imperial authority, inspecting river conservancy works on the Yellow River and Grand Canal, patronising local scholarship, and gathering intelligence on local governance. The 1711 tour was notable as the last such grand progress, reflecting both the aging emperor's energy and the growing succession crisis within the imperial family that would dominate Kangxi's final years.

sources.name: Kangxi Qijuzhu (Kangxi Emperor's Diaries of Rest and Repose)
Jonathan Spence, Emperor of China: Self-Portrait of K'ang-hsi
Michael Chang, A Court on Horseback: Imperial Touring and the Construction of Qing Rule

events.certainty_note: The sixth southern tour is documented in court records, though some scholars date elements of the tour preparations to late 1710. The tour itself is well-attested.

events.title: Charles XII of Sweden in Ottoman exile
events.description: Swedish King Charles XII remained in exile at Bender in Ottoman territory throughout 1711, having fled there after his catastrophic defeat at Poltava in 1709. He actively intrigued to draw the Ottoman Empire into war with Russia, which succeeded when the Ottomans declared war and fought the Pruth campaign. However, the Treaty of the Pruth fell far short of what Charles had hoped — he wanted the Ottomans to continue the war and help him recover his Baltic territories. The Ottomans increasingly viewed him as a troublesome guest, leading to the bizarre 'Kalabalik' incident in 1713 when they forcibly evicted him.
key_figures: Devlet II Giray (Crimean Khan, Charles's ally)

sources.name: Swedish diplomatic correspondence from Bender
Voltaire, Histoire de Charles XII (1731, based on interviews with Swedish participants)
Peter Englund, The Battle That Shook Europe: Poltava and the Birth of the Russian Empire

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in Swedish, Ottoman, and Russian sources.

events.title: First Ascertainment of the Arts and Sciences Act (Statute of Anne enforcement)
events.description: The Statute of Anne (1710), the world's first copyright statute, came into full operational effect in April 1711 when the initial registration requirements took hold and the first disputes began reaching the courts. The statute had created a 14-year copyright term (renewable once) vested in authors rather than publishers, fundamentally challenging the Stationers' Company monopoly. While the law was passed in 1710, 1711 saw its first practical effects on the London book trade, as publishers adjusted to the new legal framework and the first registrations accumulated.
key_figures: Members of the Stationers' Company (London guild of printers and booksellers)

sources.name: Statute of Anne, 8 Anne c. 19 (1710)
Ronan Deazley, On the Origin of the Right to Copy

events.certainty_note: The statute's text and implementation timeline are well-documented, though the precise pace of early enforcement is reconstructed from scattered court and trade records.

events.title: Alexander Pope publishes An Essay on Criticism
events.description: Alexander Pope published An Essay on Criticism in May 1711, a didactic poem in heroic couplets that established the 22-year-old Pope as a major literary figure. The poem, drawing on Horace, Quintilian, and Boileau, set out neoclassical principles of literary criticism and contained many lines that became proverbial in English ('A little learning is a dangerous thing,' 'To err is human, to forgive divine,' 'Fools rush in where angels fear to tread'). The work's success launched Pope's career as the dominant English poet of the 18th century.

sources.name: Alexander Pope, An Essay on Criticism (first edition, 1711)
Maynard Mack, Alexander Pope: A Life

events.certainty_note: Publication date confirmed by first edition and contemporary reviews including in The Spectator.

events.title: Rinaldo premieres — Handel's first London opera
events.description: George Frideric Handel's opera Rinaldo premiered at the Queen's Theatre, Haymarket, on 24 February 1711, becoming an immediate sensation. It was the first Italian-language opera composed specifically for the London stage, and its success established Handel as a major figure in London's musical life — a position he would hold for decades. The opera featured spectacular staging including live sparrows released on stage. Rinaldo marked the beginning of the Italian opera craze in London and initiated Handel's transformation from a German-Italian composer into a central figure of English musical culture.

sources.name: Queen's Theatre performance records and contemporary newspaper notices
Donald Burrows, Handel (biography)

events.certainty_note: Premiere date and reception documented in contemporary London press and theatre records.

events.title: Turinese physicist Giovanni Poleni builds mechanical calculator
events.description: Giovanni Poleni, professor at the University of Padua, constructed a calculating machine capable of multiplication, one of the earliest known devices of its kind. Described in his Miscellanea (published 1709–1712), the machine used a variable-toothed gear mechanism that was a significant innovation over the fixed-gear designs of Pascal and Leibniz. Though only one prototype was built and later destroyed, the design influenced subsequent work on mechanical computation and demonstrated the growing interest in applying mechanical principles to intellectual labor.

sources.name: Giovanni Poleni, Miscellanea (1709–1712)
Michael Williams, A History of Computing Technology

events.certainty_note: The machine is described in Poleni's publications, but the exact date of construction is uncertain within the 1709–1712 range. Often attributed to 1709 or 1711.

events.title: Dzungar-Qing tensions escalate in Central Asia
events.description: Tsewang Rabtan, khan of the Dzungar Khanate, continued to consolidate power in Central Asia and contest Qing influence over Tibet and the Khalkha Mongols during this period. The Dzungars controlled a vast nomadic empire spanning modern Xinjiang, Kazakhstan, and parts of Siberia, and represented the last major Inner Asian military threat to the Qing. In the early 1710s, Dzungar interference in Tibetan politics was building toward Tsewang Rabtan's invasion of Tibet in 1717, which would provoke a major Qing military response.

sources.name: Qing court records (Shilu, Veritable Records of the Kangxi reign)
Peter Perdue, China Marches West: The Qing Conquest of Central Eurasia

events.certainty_note: The broader pattern of escalating tensions is well-documented, though specific diplomatic exchanges in 1711 are less precisely dated than the military events of 1717–1720.

events.title: Yamasee and allied nations' growing grievances in the Carolina colonies
events.description: By 1711, the Yamasee and other Indigenous nations allied with South Carolina were experiencing deepening crises caused by the colonial deerskin trade: mounting debts to colonial traders, land encroachment, and the enslavement of their people to cover debts. South Carolina's use of Indigenous allies (including the Yamasee) as soldiers in the Tuscarora War of 1711–1713 intensified these grievances, as the colony demanded military service while continuing exploitative trade practices. These tensions would erupt into the Yamasee War of 1715, one of the most serious threats to British colonial survival in the Southeast.

sources.name: South Carolina colonial records and Indian trade correspondence
William Ramsey, The Yamasee War: A Study of Culture, Economy, and Conflict in the Colonial South

events.certainty_note: The buildup of grievances is well-attested in colonial records, though the process was gradual and not tied to a single 1711 event.

disconfirming_evidence: Several points of scholarly contention: (1) The traditional narrative of Catherine I saving Peter at the Pruth by offering her jewels to bribe the Grand Vizier is almost certainly apocryphal or greatly exaggerated — modern historians like Brian Davies note the Ottomans had their own strategic reasons for accepting a relatively lenient peace, including concerns about Crimean Tatar ambitions and logistical difficulties. (2) The significance of the South Sea Company's founding is often retrospectively inflated because of the 1720 bubble; at the time of its creation in 1711, it was primarily a debt-management scheme, and the actual South American trade monopoly was always more theoretical than real, given Spanish hostility. (3) The Kangxi Emperor's sixth southern tour: some scholars date this to 1707 rather than 1711, and the exact number and dating of the tours varies slightly in the secondary literature. Michael Chang's work has helped clarify the chronology. (4) Poleni's calculating machine is sometimes dated to 1709 rather than 1711; the uncertainty reflects ambiguity in his publication dates. (5) The casualty figure of ~900 dead in the Walker expedition shipwreck varies significantly across sources, from 700 to over 1,000.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1711 is rich for Europe and reasonably good for East Asia, but heavily shaped by court-sponsored and colonial perspectives. European events are over-documented relative to their global significance, partly because the War of the Spanish Succession generated vast diplomatic and military archives. The Tuscarora War and Yamasee grievances are known almost entirely through colonial records, with Indigenous perspectives reconstructed inferentially. Mughal and Ottoman sources are extensive but less widely incorporated into English-language scholarship. Sub-Saharan African, Southeast Asian, and Pacific history for this year is almost entirely absent from the record consulted, reflecting both the nature of surviving sources and biases in the training data.

graph_edges.note: Peter's Pruth campaign was part of his strategic reorientation after Poltava victory against Sweden
Joseph I's death made the Allied war aim of placing Charles on the Spanish throne politically untenable
Marlborough's dismissal signaled Britain's commitment to peace negotiations
The South Sea Company's founding created the institutional vehicle for the 1720 speculative crisis
Tuscarora War military demands on Indigenous allies intensified grievances leading to the Yamasee War
Charles XII's diplomacy in Ottoman exile helped precipitate the Ottoman-Russian war of 1711
Walker's failure preserved New France; the British would not successfully take Quebec until Wolfe in 1759
Bahadur Shah's contested reign and death were consequences of the succession instability following Aurangzeb's death
Escalating Dzungar-Qing tensions in this period culminated in Tsewang Rabtan's invasion of Tibet
Joseph I's death and Marlborough's dismissal were the twin events that ended effective Allied prosecution of the war

_meta.note: Produced via Claude Code CLI (Max subscription) before API migration
